title: Invalid submissions due to information leakage during TTT
body:
Don't train on eval tokens your model hasn't scored yet!

---

"Proper" TTT goes like this:

1. For each `1 <= t <= T`:
    1.1. Score on eval token `t`
    1.2. Adapt weights based on eval token `<= t`
---

What y'all are doing is something like this:
1. For each `1 <= t <= T`:
    1.1. Adapt weights based on eval token `<= t`
2. For each `1 <= t <= T`:
    2.1. Score on eval token `t`

But this is equivalent to appending the eval tokens to the training tokens and switching training strategies before eval! Also see: https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/152#issuecomment-4101741099

---

Potentially invalid submissions:
| PR  | comment | status |
| --- | --- | --- |
| https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/136 | TTT on half of the batch; eval on full batch | [ ] open |
| https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/152 | TTT on all eval tokens before evaluation | [x] closed |
| https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/254 | TTT on multiple parts of eval sequence for multiple epochs | [ ] open |
| https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/264 | TTT before eval | [ ] open |
| https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/338 | TTT on multiple parts of eval sequence for multiple epochs | [ ] open |
| https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/398 | TTT on all eval tokens before evaluation | [ ] Open |
| https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/421 | TTT before evals | [ ] open |
| https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/417 | TTT for multiple epochs before evals | [ ] open |
| https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/442 | TTT before evals | [ ] open |

cc @0hq 

---

Please feel free to correct me if I'm wrong.

comments.body:
I've also noticed that people are doing TTT on multiple parts of the evaluation sequence in parallel (via DDP `rank`), justifying it by only assigning each "doc" to one rank.

I'm not sure if this is comparable to the default eval strategy where we just feed all eval tokens to the model... wdyt @0hq ?

this is clearly happening bc people are using agents to monitor/use others' prs to improve their strategies. it's had an exponentially strong trickledown effect ever since a few of them made submissions that appear good at first glance with this method of basically cheating. it's a bit odd to watch.
For those using AI coding tools, I might suggest referencing the competition requirements in CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md so they are in every prompt submission. Good hunting!
How does TTT even make sense? I don’t think it should be allowed at all.
Yes! So far every sub 1.1 ish PR I've looked at is "cheating" like this. Very irritating when I'm trying to understand the true sota techniques.

> this is clearly happening bc people are using agents to monitor/use others' prs to improve their strategies. it's had an exponentially strong trickledown effect ever since a few of them made submissions that appear good at first glance with this method of basically cheating. it's a bit odd to watch.

This is exactly what is happening. I had a fully automated run get to 0.979 val BPB in a pretty convincing way (https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/517) only to later on closer inspection see that it really had bet all on on TTT annealing (and no, not the proper variety). 
Still, kind of informative to see the failure modes of the models. You can get a lot of experiments done quickly, but if they veer off in the wrong direction.. well it's one way to burn gpu hours. On the plus side, the same agent that did the solution also caught on that it might not be fully legit and it did that by reading this thread. I'm pretty sure I would have not noticed it had it not flagged it as I've been busy exploring the next thing for it to try...

This was also a conversation in the discord, so I'll try to summarize the situation here to make it abundantly clear. Here are 3 different instantiations of test-time training (TTT) I see for PRs in this repo, from least-to-most legitimate.

Claude vibe-coded website to visualize this comment: https://samacquaviva.com/projects/ttt-clarification/

**1: Train on val, then measure loss on val**

*Pseudocode:*

```python
def eval_ttt_cheat(model, val_tokens, num_epochs):
    for epoch in range(num_epochs):
        for batch in batches(val_tokens):
            sgd_step(model, loss(model, batch))
    return mean(loss(model, batch) for batch in batches(val_tokens))
```

*Valid?* 

**No, not even close**. This is *literally* training on test and obviously invalid. You could just init a large-enough random model and train for many epochs and your loss will be 0 because it will just memorize everything. 

**2: TTT auto-regressively on token stream**

*Pseudocode:*

```python
def eval_ttt_token_stream(model, val_tokens, chunk_size, context_len):
    ttt_model = deepcopy(model)
    for ci, chunk in enumerate(chunks(val_tokens, chunk_size)):
        context = val_tokens[max(0, chunk.end - context_len) : chunk.end]
        loss_per_token = ttt_model(context)
        accumulate(loss_per_token[chunk.start_in_context:])
        sgd_step(ttt_model, mean(loss_per_token[chunk.start_in_context:]))
    return total_bpb()
```

*Valid?*

Well, it depends on what is the validation set supposed to measure? The validation token stream in this competition is 50k documents from fine-web concatenated together with `BOS` tokens starting each document. So, assuming we want to model *the distribution of sequences in FineWeb*, then the validation set is 50k samples from that underlying distribution. If the competition chose 10k documents or 100k documents, the expectation of the validation loss should not change: only the variance of the estimator.

However, if we train *on the whole token stream*: this is not the case: we have more training data for the 100k document case, so the loss will *in expectation* be lower. This is clearly broken and differs from the standard definition of what an eval set is, so **this should not be allowed (I think it is under current rules)**. You could say that the point of this competition is to model this exact token sequence (50k docs) which would make this valid, but I think that is quite unnatural.

Another way to see why this should be disallowed: imagine you were trying to measure your model's "emergent capability", so you make an eval set of 50k examples of something the model was not trained to do (let's say multiply numbers). Then, if you did token-stream TTT at inference time and your model got non-zero accuracy, you cannot say it is an "emergent phenomenon" but rather that your model can learn to multiply numbers when trained on thousands of examples. Token-stream TTT is identical to just continuing to train for 1-epoch on the eval set and reporting the mean loss (in the first epoch losses for each batch are losses before training on them).

**3: TTT auto-regressively on each document, independently**

*Pseudocode*:

```python
def eval_ttt_docbased(model, val_tokens, chunk_size, context_len):
    for doc in split_at_BOS(val_tokens):
        eval_ttt_token_stream(model, doc, chunk_size, context_len)
    return total_bpb()
```

*Valid*:

**Yes! The dependency graph is identical to a non-TTT case**: token `i` in document `j` is only dependent on tokens `i'<i` in document `j`: there is no dependency on tokens ahead (the problem of just training on test) and no dependency between documents (the problem of doing TTT on the whole sequence).

I think people get hung up on the fact that it is doing backprop at test-time: it is just another operator -- no different than attending to tokens or updating some hidden state. If you attend to the label token: you are leaking the answer -- it is the same for TTT. For example, see [Energy-based Transformers](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.02092): the *entire* inference procedure is backprop. Similarly, look at LLM test-time-training papers like  [End-to-End Test-Time Training for Long Context](https://test-time-training.github.io/e2e.pdf) or [Let's (not) just put things in Context: Test-Time Training for Long-Context LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.13898): these papers all do case 3 here. [This PR](https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/77) is an example reference of case 3 in this repo. 

**Other notes**

> @leloykun: "I've also noticed that people are doing TTT on multiple parts of the evaluation sequence in parallel (via DDP rank), justifying it by only assigning each 'doc' to one rank"

This is just an efficiency improvement. Assuming you follow case 3 outlined above (as in [this PR](https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/77)), then parallelizing across documents is *identical* to doing eval over the token stream autoregressively (since each document is independent of other documents). 

> @hegdeadithyak: "How does TTT even make sense?"

Let me know if case 3 above (and referenced papers) clear it up. I agree the first two cases are incoherent.

>"I think people get hung up on the fact that it is doing backprop at test-time"

I think what rubs people the wrong way is that when you have a strict "10 minutes for training and 10 minutes for eval" rule that with TTT you are stretching the definitions of eval. IMO it's more of a problem of the rule trying to do that hard separation. But if you restrict eval to (not unreasonable) "no weights may be changed in this phase" then TTT is questionable. 

If the rule was simply "no weight changes during eval," score-first would also be banned, and the competition would just measure post-training model quality. Which is arguably cleaner.

@lukacf what about papers like [Energy-based Transformers](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.02092), [End-to-End Test-Time Training for Long Context](https://test-time-training.github.io/e2e.pdf), or [Let's (not) just put things in Context: Test-Time Training for Long-Context LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.13898)? There are whole companies (e.g. https://logicalintelligence.com/) built on these types of models.

Of course it is valid to restrict to no parameter updates, but it seems overly restrictive given that defining a clean, train-eval split version is straightforward and this is supposed to be encouraging new techniques.

Hey there, case 3 is good I saw many PRs with 1 but.

Hey yall! Sorry for the delay, balancing work and maintaining this challenge at once. I'll clarify in the README later today and appreciate the great discussion. The rules should clarify that it's indeed Case 2: The evaluation is the reconstruction error on the concatenated string of 50,000 randomly shuffled documents from the test set.

The 'token stream' intuition is correct and will help people get an intuition for what will and won't be allowed. You're allowed to use any preceding tokens from the evaluation set that you've already been tested on in any way you'd like. Also, to make things a bit simpler, I'll enforce going forward that you aren't allowed to re-order the evaluation set in any way either.

The agent submission problem is real and we're working on a Codex auto-review process that will immediately reject invalid PRs. We'll close all the invalid TTT submissions ASAP.

hey @samacqua , just for confirmation is this legal ?
```
def eval_val_ttt(args, model, device, val_tokens,
                 base_bytes_lut, has_leading_space_lut, is_boundary_token_lut):
    chunk_size = args.ttt_chunk_size
    rank       = args.ttt_lora_rank
    lr         = args.ttt_lora_lr
    adapt_steps = args.ttt_lora_steps

    tokens = val_tokens.to(device, dtype=torch.int64)
    N      = tokens.numel()

    # Freeze base model weights; only LoRA adapters are trainable
    for p in model.parameters():
        p.requires_grad_(False)

    adapters  = _attach_lora(model, rank)
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([p for a in adapters for p in a.parameters()], lr=lr)

    loss_sum    = 0.0
    token_count = 0
    byte_count  = 0.0

    model.eval()   # keep BN/dropout in eval mode; LoRA grads still flow

    pos = 0
    while pos + chunk_size + 1 <= N:
        x_chunk  = tokens[pos: pos + chunk_size].unsqueeze(0)
        y_chunk  = tokens[pos + 1: pos + chunk_size + 1].unsqueeze(0)

        #STEP 1: SCORE with current LoRA (NO GRAD for the score)
        with torch.no_grad():
            with torch.autocast(device_type="cuda", dtype=torch.bfloat16):
                logits = model.get_logits(x_chunk)
            per_tok_loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits[0].float(), y_chunk[0], reduction="none")
            chunk_loss_val = float(per_tok_loss.mean())

        loss_sum    += float(per_tok_loss.sum())
        token_count += int(y_chunk.numel())
        byte_count  += float(_count_bytes(y_chunk[0], x_chunk[0],
                                          base_bytes_lut, has_leading_space_lut, is_boundary_token_lut))

        #STEP 2: ADAPT LoRA on this chunk's loss
        #(uses only tokens ≤ pos+chunk_size, never future tokens)
        for _ in range(adapt_steps):
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            with torch.autocast(device_type="cuda", dtype=torch.bfloat16):
                adapt_loss = _forward_with_lora(model, x_chunk, y_chunk)
            adapt_loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()

        pos += chunk_size

    _detach_lora(model)
    for p in model.parameters():
        p.requires_grad_(True)
    model.train()

    val_loss        = loss_sum / max(token_count, 1)
    bits_per_token  = val_loss / math.log(2.0)
    tokens_per_byte = token_count / max(byte_count, 1.0)
    return val_loss, float(bits_per_token * tokens_per_byte)
```

Are implementations like #611 legal? I'd have to imagine they're not—they're getting dramatically lower scores through re-scoring tokens after the model already trained on them. There's a new host of these types of submissions flooding in.

It seems there's been a new scheme going on where people are using a score-every-epoch approach where they score all tokens in a document, train on them, and then rescore the same tokens and keep the minimum NLL across epochs. This doesn't seem to fit the goal of the challenge; it's really just exploiting eval and doesn't have any real innovation imo. 


- LoRA TTT with score-every-epoch (#596, #568, #620, #512, #548, etc.) — the questionable method
@himanalot Can't speak for every TTT scheme, but agreed that the score-every-epoch-and-keep-the-lowest-loss is equivalent to training on the val set, and is disallowed in my book.

@valerio-oai

Personally I would prefer that TTT was written into the rules as explicitly disallowed, it wastes $ outside of training on compute. I called out whether or not my approach would be 'legal' in my PR and seems like the consensus is _no_. 

I can try another seed run with `TTT_EPOCHS=1` to be fully compliant but I might need to apply for some more compute at this point. I'd expect to gain around ~0.04-0.06 BPB hopefully scoring around 1.10-1.12 but sounds like I should just scrap TTT all together? 

My PR: https://github.com/openai/parameter-golf/pull/620

@robinojw I see your point, but the reason we give the 10-min eval time is so folks can have interesting test-time approaches, so TTT is a valid approach in theory, but very easy to unintentionally leak val data into. There's still a long time to go, and I'm sure approaches will start to crystallize eventually.
